response:
# Top 3 changes for `src/billing.ts`

## 1. Break up `generateInvoice` (crime_00002 — God Function, severity 0.9)
204 lines (L50–253), 3.4× the 60-line domain threshold. Almost every other billing.ts finding lives *inside* this function: 7 of the file's Date calls (crime_00009), 4 TODOs (crime_00035), the L39 hidden rule (crime_00017), and the "enterprise" literal scatter (crime_00012). Splitting it into named helpers — `assembleLineItems`, `applyDiscounts`, `computeTaxes`, `persistAndDispatch` — also pulls the file under the 300-line God File threshold (crime_00016) and gives each section a testable seam. This is the highest-leverage edit because the smaller findings collapse alongside it.

## 2. Rename `calculateRefundPreview` + encode the L39 rule (crime_00013 + crime_00017)
These are one bug wearing two hats. The name promises a pure preview (`calculate…Preview`), but the body calls `saveRefundAudit` and `sendRefundEmail` (severity 0.55, agent_risk 0.52). Directly above it sits a comment carrying load-bearing policy that the code doesn't encode: *"Only owners can refund enterprise plans unless billing support approves."* An agent will read the name, assume safe-to-invoke, call it speculatively, and quietly fire an email + audit row — while bypassing the owner/support gate that lives only in prose. Two-step fix: extract the pure calculation into `previewRefund` (no side effects), put the side-effecting path behind `commitRefund(actor, plan)` that takes an explicit authorization context, and turn the comment into a typed guard (`assertCanRefund(actor, plan)`).

## 3. Inject a clock — eliminate the 7 direct Date calls (crime_00009)
7 `Date.now()` / `new Date()` calls between L57 and L273 (agent_risk 0.53), in a function that *generates invoices* — i.e., produces financial artifacts where the timestamp is part of the output. Tests can't pin a moment without monkey-patching, which is why `billing.test.ts` already has a zero-assertion test (crime_00011) and a weak-matcher test (crime_00030) — the suite can't actually verify dated output. Thread a `clock: { now(): Date }` (or `now: () => Date`) through the call site, default to a real clock at the CLI boundary, and pass a frozen `Date` in tests. This unblocks meaningful assertions on invoice issue/due dates and removes the strongest temporal coupling left after #1 splits the function.
